summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – COSTS – Where the de facto husband (“husband”) seeks costs in a fixed sum against the de facto wife (“wife”) – Where the Court had dismissed the wife’s Response to the application for costs on the basis of no reasonable prospects of success and default in appearance – Where the Court took into account an affidavit of the wife over the objections of the husband – Where the wife was a self-represented litigant at final hearing – Where the wife was wholly unsuccessful in her application for property division – Where the wife unreasonably rejected offers for settlement – Where the Court is satisfied the wife's conduct during proceedings unnecessarily increased costs for the husband – Where the wife has been non-compliant with court orders – Where the husband failed to establish a claim for indemnity costs – Orders for the wife to make partial payment of the husband’s costs in a fixed sum.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

HARPER J:

These are property proceedings under Pt VIIIAB of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) (“the Act”) between the de facto wife, Ms Julien (“wife”), who was the applicant in the substantive proceedings, and the de facto husband, Mr Perrin (“husband”) who was the respondent in the substantive proceedings.
On 18 September 2025, I delivered final judgment: Julien & Perrin (No 9) [2025] FedCFamC1F 655. The relevant procedural history and background to these proceedings are set out in that judgment, I will not repeat what I have set out in there unless necessary for this judgment.
On 16 October 2025, the husband filed an Application in a Proceeding for costs orders in his favour against the wife, supported by two affidavits. One of these affidavits was sworn by a solicitor concerning time costing, and exhibiting tax invoices.
Procedural orders for any evidence and written submissions were made on 20 November 2025.
The husband filed written submissions on 8 December 2025, a tender bundle on 19 January 2026 and reply submissions on 19 January 2026.
The wife filed an affidavit on 5 January 2026. No submissions were received from the wife.
On 5 January 2026, the wife also filed a Response to the husband’s application for costs. She sought a range of orders, including a stay of the costs application.
On 2 February 2026, the application for costs was listed for Mention. The wife, a self-represented litigant at final hearing and at the date of Mention, failed to appear without reasonable explanation. The Court was satisfied the orders sought in her Response were beyond the Court’s jurisdiction or entirely misconceived, and ordered her Response be dismissed as having no reasonable prospect of success and separately in the default in her appearance. No further application has been made by the wife.
Accordingly, I have taken account of the husband’s submissions and evidence. Over the husband’s objections, I have taken the wife’s affidavit into account.

ORDERS SOUGHT AS TO COSTS

The husband seeks orders for:
(a) costs fixed in the sum of $1,056,417.32 as follows:
(i) for the period from the date of filing of the application on 15 November 2022 to 31 March 2025 in the sum of $257,770.54;

(ii) for the period from 1 April 2025 onwards on an indemnity basis in the sum of $700,491.91; and

(iii) reimbursements for experts totalling $98,154.87;

(b) in the alternative, costs fixed in the sum of $699,506.96, inclusive of reimbursements for experts totalling $98,154.87;

(c) costs of his application for costs in the sum of $13,057.24; and

(d) certification for counsel and senior counsel.

PRINCIPLES REGARDING COSTS

The amendments to the Act brought into effect by the Family Law Amendment Act 2024 (Cth) apply to these proceedings.
Section 114UB is the central provision concerning costs orders and replaces repealed s 117. Since s 114UB is in materially the same terms as the repealed s 117, I am satisfied that the body of authority decided under s 117 remains applicable.
In proceedings to which the Act applies, the starting point is that each party shall bear his or her own costs (s 114UB(1)). If, however, the Court is of the opinion that there are justifying circumstances, the Court may make such order as to costs and security for costs as the Court considers just (s 114UB(2)), being costs in the conventional sense, that is, as indemnity for another’s liability for professional fees and out of pocket expenses reasonably incurred in the litigation.
The Court has a wide discretion, which is to be exercised judicially (Parke & The Estate of the Late A Parke [2016] FamCAFC 248; (2016) FLC 93-748; Atkins & Hunt (Costs) [2017] FamCAFC 131). When considering what, if any, order for costs should be made, the Court must have regard to the factors set out in s 114UB(3);
(3) In considering what costs order (if any) should be made, the court must have regard to the following:
(a) the financial circumstances of each party to the proceedings;

(b) whether any party to the proceedings is receiving assistance by way of legal aid in respect of the proceedings and, if so, the terms of the grant of the assistance to that party;

(c) the conduct of the parties to the proceedings in relation to the proceedings including, without limiting paragraphs (a) and (b), the conduct of the parties in relation to pleadings, particulars, discovery, inspection, directions to answer questions, admissions of facts, production of documents and similar matters, and in relation to their duty of disclosure under subsection 71B(1), 90RI(1) or 90YJA(1);

(d) whether the proceedings were necessitated by the failure of a party to the proceedings to comply with previous orders of the court;

(e) whether any party to the proceedings has been wholly unsuccessful in the proceedings;

(f) whether a party to the proceedings has made an offer in writing to the other party to the proceedings to settle the proceedings and the terms of any such offer;

(g) such other matters as the court considers relevant.
According to long standing authority, no one factor in s 114UB(3) has priority, nor must more than one factor be satisfied; rather, any one factor may be sufficient (Prantage & Prantage (Costs) [2014] FamCA 850 at [12]) to justify departing from s 114UB(1) and making an award of costs.
I have had regard to all the factors set forth in s 114UB(3).

DISCUSSION

Section 114UB(3)(a) – the financial circumstances of the parties

The final orders made no alteration to the parties’ property on the basis it was not just and equitable. The husband retained approximately 99 per cent of the net asset pool of $35,697,142.
As noted in the final judgment at [261], the wife has a business which according to her could have received an investment of $30,000,000. However, there is no evidence about this possibility coming to fruition.
I accept the husband is in a significantly superior financial position to the wife. I am not satisfied the financial circumstances of the parties justify a costs order in the husband’s favour.

Section 114UB(3)(c) – the conduct of the parties

The husband set out extensive complaints in his material about the wife’s conduct increasing legal costs. The relevant conduct is broadly summarised as follows:
(a) the wife’s series of unsuccessful interlocutory applications;

(b) the wife’s pursuit of an equitable claim and abandonment of same at hearing;

(c) the wife’s conduct in relation to the extension of time to nominate an IT expert, necessitating the husband filing an interlocutory application;

(d) the wife’s conduct in relation to an extension of time to serve a list of privileged documents, necessitating the husband filing an interlocutory application; and

(e) the wife’s failure to comply with a series of court orders.

It is not necessary to set out each complaint in detail, I have had regard to the relevant evidence.
The husband argued that the wife was a self-represented litigant from 23 June 2025 through to final hearing, so she did not incur legal fees from bringing groundless applications, but the husband did. He argued her series of interlocutory applications required court appearances and lengthened final hearing from five days to eight days. He submitted the Court should find her conduct was designed to cause him to incur substantial legal fees in the intent to extract better offers of settlement.
As I observed in the final judgment, the trial did not proceed in an orderly fashion on account of a number of procedural applications of the wife being heard just prior to and throughout final hearing (at [75]). I formed the view the wife spent much of the Court’s time persisting with irrelevant allegations against the husband, expressions of grievance based upon unfounded perceptions and a desire to delay the finalisation of the proceedings by adjournment (at [9]).
The husband argued the wife failed to comply with procedural orders for the filing of material and engagement with a single expert. He claims this put him to extra expense in relation to late filed trial material, and pursuing orders to ensure expert valuations were ready in time for final hearing.
The wife continues to be non-compliant with court orders made during trial on 15 August 2025 and extended on 18 August 2025, to deliver the “laptop” to the Court. The laptop and its contents were a source of significant contention throughout proceedings about which the wife made a range of unsuccessful allegations and applications. The husband claimed the wife’s failure to comply extended final hearing and necessitated him to file an Application in a Proceeding seeking access to the laptop, and to amend his final relief sought to include orders about the laptop. As at the date of this judgment, the wife remains non-compliant with outstanding orders of the Court for delivery of the laptop.
I am satisfied the wife failed to conduct proceedings to discharge her duty to act consistently with the overarching purpose and objectives of the Act, including to facilitate the just resolution of disputes as quickly, inexpensively and efficiently as possible (s 95 and s 96 of the Act).

Section 114UB(3)(e) – whether either party has been wholly unsuccessful

The wife was wholly unsuccessful in her application for property adjustment.

Section 114UB(3)(f) – offers in writing

The husband made four offers of settlement to the wife on the following dates:
(a) 10 February 2025 (“First Offer”);

(b) 25 March 2025 (“Second Offer”);

(c) 11 August 2025 (“Third Offer”); and

(d) 17 August 2025 (“Fourth Offer”).

I have had regard to the offers made as set out in the evidence. It is not necessary to set out each offer in detail. I will refer to them to the extent necessary. It is however plain that each offer proposed an outcome in which the wife did substantially better than the ultimate result.
In Magnus & Sandri (No 5) [2024] FedCFamC1F 762 at [41]–[43], I set out the following principles in relation to the reasonableness of an offer and of any failure to accept which I venture to repeat:
The reasonableness of an offer and of any failure to accept is in part to be assessed by reference to a settled balance sheet and an objective assessment of the state of knowledge of the offeree at the time the offer was made. In Pennisi & Pennisi [1997] FamCA 39; (1997) FLC 92-774 (“Pennisi”) at 84,547, when referring to s 117(2A)(f), the Full Court said:
The husband’s submissions refer to Robinson and Higginbotham (1991) FLC 92‑209 as authority for the proposition that the fact that an offer just exceeds the award is no bar to an order for costs. Equally, however, it is only one of the factors to be considered under s 117(2A) and it is not the law that an offer of greater or equivalent value to that which results from the Court will lead to an order for costs in favour of the offeror – Harris and Harris (1991) FLC 92‑254.

We would also add that just because an offer is marginally less than the amount ordered by a Court does not mean that it is not a factor to be taken into account in determining whether costs should be awarded.

....

Offers must be seen in the context of the case and the extent of the offeree’s knowledge of the parties’ financial circumstances while the offer is live. In the family law jurisdiction, it is not uncommon to find relationships where one party, often the wife, has significantly less grasp of the parties’ financial arrangements, or the financial circumstances are so complex that it would be premature to accept an offer. There are also cases where the contents of the offer are in themselves the subject of disputed value and legitimate subject matter for determination. These and other features of the context of offers must be taken into account when considering whether it was reasonable or not to accept an offer, no matter how close to the ultimate result the offer may be.
The Full Court has endorsed these views in Conrad & Conrad (2020) 61 Fam LR 301 at [25]–[30] and Raymond & Raymond (No 2) [2024] FedCFamC1A 92 at [11].
Here the financial arrangements were complex and the wife had a significantly lesser grasp of their detail as at November 2019, especially concerning the husband’s share trading. It is also not uncommon that a reasonable understanding of the overall composition of the balance sheet between the parties relies upon determinations made by the Court and expert evidence, which may substantially affect the pool of assets sheet upon which the Court relies in making property division orders.
The terms of the First Offer provided for the husband to pay the wife the sum of $250,000 and for the wife to return the laptop to the husband. The offer was accepted by the wife’s then legal representatives on 27 February 2025. Draft consent orders were exchanged between the parties’ lawyers on 4 March 2025, which the husband signed on the same date at the request of the wife’s lawyers. However, the wife failed to sign the consent orders, without explanation to the husband, and the offer expired on 10 March 2025.
In her affidavit the wife claimed that she asked for clarification of the husband’s offer, in particular an explanation of the connection between the proposed payment of $250,000 and return of the laptop. The evidence does not support the wife actually sought any such explanation. But even if true, this does not make the wife’s failure to accept the First Offer by not executing the consent orders any less unreasonable. The wife’s claim was for property adjustment between de facto spouses. She claimed the laptop was central to the proceedings. But as the final judgment reveals, she was unable to demonstrate why this was so. In my view it was peripheral and the wife’s fixation on it the source of confusion and delay. Accordingly, her reasons for not participating in the First Offer so as to bring the litigation to an end, was extraneous to the issues in the proceedings.
The First Offer was made about six months prior to the commencement of final hearing in August 2025, after proceedings had been initiated in 2022. While it is possible the wife might have had a lesser knowledge of the parties’ financial position than the husband at the time, she had legal representation and accepted the offer, suggesting that it was not premature to accept an offer in terms as proposed by the husband.
The First Offer was clearly reasonable. The wife’s application for property division was wholly dismissed at final hearing, so it is undeniable she would have been in a better position had she not resiled from her acceptance of the First Offer. The final orders ultimately provide for the release of the laptop to the husband, though as mentioned, the wife remains non-compliant with outstanding orders to deliver the device to the Court. I am satisfied that had she not resiled from the First Offer, proceedings would have been brought to a close many months earlier to the financial benefit of the wife and with considerable costs savings for the husband.
The Second Offer was made on the same terms as the First Offer, save for the payment sum being reduced to $225,000. The offer was open for 28 days, during which time the wife had legal representation, but the husband received no response from the wife. Again, for the same reasons as the First Offer, the Second Offer would have provided the wife a better outcome than resulted at final hearing, as well as saving costs to the husband.
The wife did not have legal representation at the time of the Third Offer and Fourth Offer. The Third Offer, was an open offer made on the first day of final hearing, offered no payment sum to the wife, but provided for each party to pay their own costs and for the wife to return the laptop to the husband. The wife did not respond to the offer.
The Fourth Offer made prior to the sixth day of final hearing, offered terms that included a payment of $250,000 to the wife, return of the laptop to the husband and for the parties to pay their own costs. The wife rejected the offer. Again, acceptance of the Fourth Offer would have provided a significantly better outcome for the wife and for costs savings to the husband. By the time the Fourth Offer was made, considerable time had been used up and considerable expense had been incurred by the husband.
I am satisfied that the wife’s response to each of the offers was unreasonable.

CONCLUSION ON JUSTIFYING CIRCUMSTANCES

The wife was wholly unsuccessful in her application for property adjustment. I am satisfied that if she had accepted the husband’s First Offer in February 2025, or the subsequent offers, the outcome would have been more favourable to her, and that it was unreasonable for her to refuse the offers. I am also satisfied her conduct during proceedings unnecessarily increased costs for the husband and wasted Court time and resources on largely irrelevant issues. I am satisfied these matters justify an award of some costs in favour of the husband.

INDEMNITY COSTS

Indemnity costs are awarded only in exceptional circumstances and are rare (Moorcroft & Moorcroft [2020] FamCAFC 83; (2020) 60 Fam LR 361; Harris & Dewell and Anor (No 2) [2018] FamCAFC 180; (2018) FLC 93-863).
In Munday v Bowman (1997) FLC 92-784 at 84,660 Holden CJ set out five circumstances in which an award of indemnity costs might be justified, the most relevant circumstances in this case arising from the arguments of the husband are misconduct, undue prolongation of a case and an imprudent refusal of an offer to compromise.
The husband argues the circumstances are such that the Court should award indemnity costs in his favour for the period from 1 April 2025 onwards, being a date after the wife reneged on the First Offer but prior to expiry of the Second Offer. He seeks indemnity costs from 1 April 2025 onwards in the sum of $700,491.91.
I have already discussed the wife’s conduct above, and I am satisfied her actions not only wasted the time of the Court but caused the husband to incur unnecessary costs disproportionate to the issues in dispute. The wife’s rejection of the husband’s First Offer, and subsequent offers, was unreasonable.
The award of indemnity costs is exceptional. In my view, the circumstances described above do not fall within the exceptional category. The wife was self-represented by June 2025. Self-represented litigants are unfortunate. They often distract themselves with extraneous or irrelevant matters.

CONCLUSION

Having said that, the husband should not bear the cost of the wife’s delinquency. I consider that further expense should be avoided by fixing, pursuant to the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth), a specific amount to avoid any further costs. I conclude that a costs order against the wife fixed in the amount of $699,506.96, being the husband’s alternative proposal including reimbursement for experts, is fair and appropriate to adequately compensate the husband to the disadvantage of what otherwise would have been the position of the wife in the absence of her delinquency (Oshlack v Richmond River Council [1998] HCA 11; (1998) 193 CLR 72 at 89, [44]).
There should also be a costs order for the husband’s application for costs fixed in the sum of $13,057.
The husband sought certification for counsel, including senior counsel, on the basis the issues in the proceedings warranted briefing senior counsel. Since I have fixed the amount for costs, assessment and therefore certification, is unnecessary.
For the foregoing reasons I am satisfied the orders at the commencement of this judgment should be made.
